result:
The rain doesn’t fall in this city; it arrives sideways, like gossip. I’ve lived here long enough to stop opening umbrellas. Instead I let the weather rewrite me. My coat becomes darker, my thoughts louder, my memories slightly rearranged by water. There’s a strange comfort in being edited by something that doesn’t care who you are.

Last night I watched an old man feed pigeons with crumbs from a croissant he clearly couldn’t afford to share. The birds didn’t thank him. They never do. Yet he kept breaking off smaller and smaller pieces until the pastry was gone and his hands were empty. That moment felt more religious than anything I’ve seen inside stained glass.

I keep thinking about how most of what we love will outlive us by decades or centuries. The song you can’t stop humming will still be playing in grocery stores after you’re dust. The joke you told your daughter will make her laugh again twenty years from now when she least expects it. We are temporary conductors of something far more permanent than ourselves.

Sometimes I leave the house with no destination just to practice being a stranger. I ride the subway to the last stop, walk through neighborhoods where no one knows my name, and pretend I’m a character in somebody else’s novel. It’s the only time my anxiety shuts up.

The truth is I don’t have a grand philosophy. I only have small devotions: good coffee, bad puns, the particular silence that falls after a thunderstorm, and the stubborn belief that kindness still matters even when it changes nothing visible.

(Word count: 248)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
